PR #45595: [XLA:GPU] Support VA remapping of parameter buffer allocations

Imported from GitHub PR https://github.com/openxla/xla/pull/45595

## 📝 Summary of Changes

This PR extends command-buffer virtual-address remapping to entry-computation parameter allocations. Parameter buffers remain caller-owned, so each execution aliases their physical memory into stable VA-reservation addresses instead of transferring ownership to the VMM allocator.

- Adds `ExecutionScope::ResolveParameterBuffer`; the base implementation returns the caller buffer, while the VA-remapping implementation installs and returns the stable reservation alias.
- Renames the `PrepareReservation` hook to `Prepare` and makes `ExecuteWithBufferAllocations` accept mutable `BufferAllocations`, allowing addresses to be restored after execution.
- Tracks per-execution parameter aliases with `RecordStepAlias`.
- Releases those aliases after every execution and rewrites remapped entries back to their caller-owned addresses before teardown and result handling. The execution-scope destructor provides cleanup for error paths.
- Adds `AddVaRemappedAllocationForTesting` and a host-only fake VMM allocator to exercise the parameter-remapping path without requiring a GPU.

This PR is stacked on #45567, **[XLA:GPU] Split command buffer VA remapping into its own allocator class**. It is part 1 of 3 splitting the former #45568 implementation into reviewable changes; #45596 adds output-buffer remapping and #45597 adds the `SKIP_PROFILED` mode.

## 🎯 Justification

`SKIP_TEMP` stabilizes only preallocated temporary buffers, but profiled command-buffer update policies also need to stabilize caller-owned parameters whose physical addresses remain constant across executions. Parameter ownership prevents allocating them directly through the command-buffer reservation.

Creating a per-execution alias gives recorded command buffers a stable VA while preserving the caller's original address and ownership. Restoring the original address after execution ensures teardown and result handling never observe a reservation address.

## 🚀 Kind of Contribution

✨ New Feature

## 📊 Benchmark (for Performance Improvements)

Not applicable as a standalone change. This PR adds parameter-remapping support but does not select it in a production update mode. Performance evaluation belongs to the final `SKIP_PROFILED` policy in #45597.

## 🧪 Unit Tests:

- Added host-only `gpu_executable_va_remap_allocator_test.cc` coverage using a fake VMM allocator.
- Verifies that a caller-owned parameter receives the same stable reservation address across executions.
- Verifies persistent allocation-index handling, post-execution restoration to the caller address, and release of each per-execution mapping.
- Covers cleanup behavior without requiring a GPU.

## 🧪 Execution Tests:

No standalone execution test is added because no production update mode selects parameter allocations in this PR. End-to-end selection and execution coverage is provided by the stacked `SKIP_PROFILED` change in #45597.

a0de77a1e85642e79d8b9f5d4d988402280ecfbb by Shawn Wang <shawnw@nvidia.com>:

[XLA:GPU] Support VA remapping of parameter buffer allocations

Allow command buffer VA remapping to give stable reservation addresses
to entry-computation-parameter allocations. Parameter buffers are owned
by the caller, so they are aliased into the VA reservation with Map()
each execution instead of being allocated through it:

- GpuExecutableBufferAllocator::ExecutionScope gains a
  ResolveParameterBuffer hook (base returns the caller buffer), and
  ExecuteWithBufferAllocations now takes a mutable BufferAllocations so
  subclasses can rewrite entries after execution. The Prepare hook is
  renamed from PrepareReservation.
- GpuExecutableVaRemapAllocator records each installed alias
  (RecordStepAlias) and, after every execution, UnMaps the aliases and
  rewrites the remapped BufferAllocations entries back to the
  caller-owned addresses so TearDown and result handling never see
  reservation addresses (ReleaseStepAliases; the scope destructor is a
  safety net for error paths).
- No production update mode selects parameter allocations yet
  (SKIP_TEMP remaps only preallocated temp buffers); the
  AddVaRemappedAllocationForTesting seam plus a host-only fake VMM
  allocator (gpu_executable_va_remap_allocator_test.cc) exercise the
  new path: stable reservation address across executions, persistent
  allocation indices, post-execution rewrite, and mapping release.

Support shaped types in SimplifyArithPass

Originally the pass was written for the Fusion Emitters pipeline. Now that it
is also used for CPU block codegen, we can have shaped types as well.

This change updates SimplifyArithPass to handle shaped types (vectors and
tensors) safely across several rewrite patterns:
- In FindNarrowestValueInChain, use mlir::getElementTypeOrSelf to get the
  element type before querying bitwidths. Direct calls to
  Type::getIntOrFloatBitWidth() on ShapedType failed an MLIR assertion.
- In RewriteMinMaxFWithNaNPropagation and RewriteCmpI, construct
  DenseElementsAttr constants when operating on ShapedType operands.
- Add lit tests for shaped types and scalar NaN propagation mode in
  SimplifyArithPass.

[PjRt-IFRT] Reject XLA HloShardingV3 (NamedSharding) format in IFRT `HloShardingSerDes` serialization

XLA `HloShardingV3` (NamedSharding) format is currently not version compatible across IFRT SerDes versions as the version compatibility contract has not been established around `HloShardingV3`, and `HloShardingV3` is intended to be used within the local XLA compiler. Allowing `HloShardingSerDes` to serialize `HloShardingV3` format can cause potential compatibility errors when communicating across different binary versions or using persistent artifacts such as model exports.

This change updates `HloShardingSerDes::Serialize` to explicitly reject `HloSharding` containing `HloShardingV3` format, returning an `InvalidArgumentError`.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 948702212

[IFRT] Copy serialized Cord to std::string during deserialization to avoid ASan buffer overflow

MLIR Lexer (used by the text/assembly parser) assumes the input buffer is null-terminated.
When parsing text-based StableHLO during deserialization (e.g. on invalid input in tests), `deserializePortableArtifact` receives a non-null-terminated `absl::string_view` from `absl::Cord::Flatten()`.
If an identifier ends exactly at the buffer boundary, the lexer reads past the end of the buffer, causing an ASan `stack-buffer-overflow`.

To fix this safely, we copy the serialized data to a `std::string` to guarantee null-termination before passing it to MLIR parsing. Since this only happens during program deserialization (non-hot path), the performance impact is expected to be low, but we will need to do a follow-up if the extra cost turns out to be too high.

[PJRT] Add UndonatableCommonPjRtBuffer: a hold-free PjRtBuffer for non-donating workloads.

Serving/inference buffers (like request inputs and loaded variables) follow a write-once, read-many lifecycle and are never donated. For these workloads, the hold-tracking and usage-event machinery in `CommonPjRtBuffer` causes unnecessary overhead and locking on the hot path.

`UndonatableCommonPjRtBuffer` extends `xla::PjRtBuffer` directly and strips away this tracking state.

Key mechanics:
  * Safe Construction & RAII Lifetime:** Enforces non-null `raw_buffer` invariants upon construction. Buffer lifetime is strictly tied to C++ object destruction, deferring release (`DecrefAfter`) until all pending definition events resolve without tracking dormant utilization holds.
  * Lock-Free Accessors & Retrieval:** Because `raw_buffer_` stays valid throughout the C++ object's lifetime, `AcquireRawBufferRef()` and metadata accessors (`memory_space`, `device`, `client`) are completely lock-free (`mu_` is bypassed), avoiding mutex contention during high-throughput inference dispatch.
  * No-op `Delete()`:** `Delete()` is an explicit no-op (`IsDeleted()` returns `false`) and early release is disabled to prevent accidental use-after-delete races in asynchronous callbacks.

PR #45567: [XLA:GPU] Split command buffer VA remapping into its own allocator class

Imported from GitHub PR https://github.com/openxla/xla/pull/45567

## 📝 Summary of Changes

This PR separates the command-buffer virtual-address remapping implementation from `GpuExecutableBufferAllocator` into a dedicated `GpuExecutableVaRemapAllocator` subclass. It is a structural refactor of the existing `SKIP_TEMP` behavior; no functional change is intended.

- `GpuExecutableBufferAllocator` is now the common base implementation for allocation resolution and the `ALWAYS_UPDATE` policy. It also remains the runtime fallback for `SKIP_TEMP` when VA remapping is unavailable.
- `ExecutionScope` is now polymorphic and is returned as `std::unique_ptr<ExecutionScope>`. Protected hooks such as `PrepareReservation` and `AllocateTransientBuffer` provide the extension points needed by address-policy-specific subclasses.
- The new `GpuExecutableVaRemapAllocator` owns the `SKIP_TEMP` implementation:
  - per-executor remapping state and synchronization;
  - deterministic VA reservation sizing and layout;
  - VMM-backed allocation of command-buffer-referenced temporary buffers;
  - persistent allocation indices combining constants with VA-remapped temporary allocations; and
  - synchronization of pending VMM operations during allocator teardown.
- `GpuExecutableBufferAllocator::Create` selects the base allocator for `ALWAYS_UPDATE` and the VA-remapping subclass for `SKIP_TEMP`.
- GPU executable and PJRT call sites now use the factory and pointer-owned execution scopes.
- The BUILD target includes the new source/header pair and retains the direct dependencies required by both implementations.
- VMM test logging now enables both allocator translation units so existing assertions continue to observe the VA-reservation messages after the code move.

This PR is stacked on #45130, **[XLA:GPU] Update command buffers when allocation policy changes**. The follow-up policy is implemented separately in #45568.

## 🎯 Justification

The base allocator previously mixed general buffer-allocation mechanics with one specific VMM address-stability policy. Splitting those responsibilities makes the default path easier to reason about and gives additional update policies a narrow place to implement reservation, allocation, and execution behavior without further expanding the base class.

This cleanup is primarily preparation for profiled command-buffer allocation policies. Existing GPU command-buffer workloads should retain the same `ALWAYS_UPDATE` and `SKIP_TEMP` behavior.

## 📊 Benchmark (for Performance Improvements)

Not applicable. This is a behavior-preserving refactor and makes no performance claim.

- Existing assertions continue to cover the persistent-allocation sets for `ALWAYS_UPDATE` and `SKIP_TEMP`, including the non-VMM fallback path.

[XLA:GPU] Refactor DotMerger to use DotOperandDims and ShapeTracker.

This change enhances the capabilities of the DotMerger pass. Instead of relying on specific patterns like DotDecomposer's normal form, the new implementation uses `DotOperandDims` to track and categorize dimensions (Batch, Contracting, NonContracting) and `ShapeTracker` to generate the necessary transformation instructions (transpose, reshape, bitcast) to align operand shapes for merging.

Key improvements include:
-   Support for merging dots with different layouts on the shared or concatenated operands.
-   Ability to handle dots with arbitrary batch, contracting, and non-contracting dimension orders.
-   Merging of dots even when the non-shared operands have multiple non-contracting dimensions.
-   Improved handling of dependencies by merging only mutually unreachable dots within an equivalence class.
-   Simplification of consumer chains (transposes, reshapes, bitcasts) after the merged dot's output is sliced.

The equivalence classes are now built based on the shared operand's source instruction and the categorized dimensions at that source, allowing for more flexible grouping. The merging process involves transforming the operands to a common shape/layout before concatenation and then creating a single large dot. The results are then sliced and potentially transformed back to match the original dot outputs.

PR #45646: [XLA:GPU] Restrict generic Triton FP4 codegen

Imported from GitHub PR https://github.com/openxla/xla/pull/45646

📝 Summary of Changes
Restrict generic Triton codegen for `f4E2M1FN` values to storage-preserving paths that terminate at scaled-dot data operands. Generic FP4 arithmetic and FP4 fusion outputs are rejected before XTile/Triton lowering. This is another PR in the decomposition of the larger scaled-dot fix series: https://github.com/openxla/xla/pull/44223.

🎯 Justification
Packed FP4 scaled-dot support carries logical `f4E2M1FN` values through XTile as physical `i8` storage. That is required for scaled-dot operands, but it must not imply generic FP4 Triton codegen support.

Earlier https://github.com/openxla/xla/pull/45082 introduces test regressions: `IsTritonSupportedInstruction` rejects generic `f4e2m1fn` output, while XTile/Triton lowering could succeed for some generic FP4 paths ScaledDotOperandTypes/f4e2m1fn_SymbolicTiling, ScaledDotOperandTypes/f4e2m1fn_ExperimentalTiling). If current PR merged first, issues in github.com/openxla/xla/pull/45082 are expected to be fixable with a small follow up commit.

This PR makes the codegen boundary explicit: FP4 arrays may flow through storage-preserving ops such as parameter, bitcast, copy, reshape, slice, and transpose only when the path feeds scaled-dot operands 0 or 1. Other FP4 users or FP4 fusion outputs return `InvalidArgument`.

🐛 Bug Fix, 🧪 Tests

[xla:gpu] Improve dot fusion cost model by incorporating hardware launch waves

The existing pipelined loop cost model assumes perfect, continuous overlapping
between compute and memory operations. However, hardware executes in discrete
"waves" bounded by SM counts, available shared memory, and thread capacities.
When a kernel's resource requirements prevent all threadblocks from launching
simultaneously, the pipeline is interrupted at wave boundaries, causing overhead.

This CL introduces hardware wave awareness into the pipelined cost model to
prevent it from under-predicting the execution time of configurations that
require multiple waves.

Key changes:
* Abstract a new `CalculateHardwareLaunchWaves` metric to predict the number
  of discrete execution waves, accounting for shared memory and warp occupancy
  limits across physical SMs.
* Introduce `CalculatePipelinedLoopTimeWithLaunchWaves`, which applies the
  pipelined execution model on a per-wave basis. This models the pipeline
  drain/fill overhead at wave boundaries.
* Iron out precision drops and mitigate potential divide-by-zero hazards when
  evaluating degenerate 0-block layouts.
* Add unit tests to explicitly verify wave computation scaling and assert the
  correctness of per-wave pipeline bounding logic across different memory limits.

PR #44403: [XLA:CPU] onednn_threadpool: cap num_workers at n in parallel_for

Imported from GitHub PR https://github.com/openxla/xla/pull/44403

📝 Summary of Changes
`OneDnnThreadPool::parallel_for` (xla/backends/cpu/runtime/onednn/onednn_threadpool.h)  was passing `thread_pool_- NumThreads()` as `num_workers` regardless of `n`. Before   #42581, `Worker::Parallelize` had an entry-point clamp (`num_workers = std::min(num_work_items, num_workers)`) that silently fixed this up. #42581 replaced that clamp with a branch that triggers when `num_workers > num_work_items`:

  ```cpp
  if (num_workers > num_work_items) {
    num_partitions = ceil(num_work_items / 8);
    num_workers   = std::min(num_workers, num_partitions);
  }
  ```

  For oneDNN's small-n dispatches (reorders, packing, eltwise/softmax tiles), this collapses parallelism to ceil(n/8) cores.
  eg: On a 56-thread host, n = 20 drops from 20 active cores to 3; n <= 8 falls through to a fully serial fallback. Multiplied across the many small primitives, this causes significant regression across multiple models when built with `build_with_onednn_async=true` and `XLA_FLAGS=--xla_cpu_experimental_onednn_custom_call=true`

  Fix
  Cap num_workers at n at the caller, matching the pattern already used in xla/backends/cpu/runtime/kernel.cc and xla/backends/cpu/runtime/convolution_lib.h:
  ```cpp
  const size_t num_workers = std::min<size_t>(static_cast<size_t>(n),
                                              thread_pool_->NumThreads());
  ```

  With num_workers <= num_work_items, the call takes the else branch (num_partitions = num_workers) and behaves like pre-#42581. For n >= NumThreads(), the cap is a no-op and the heap/cacheline win from #42581 is fully preserved.

🎯 Justification
It fixes the significant regression (25%-60%) across multiple models run on CPU with onednn

PR #45130: [XLA:GPU] Trigger command buffers update when persistent allocations set changed.

Imported from GitHub PR https://github.com/openxla/xla/pull/45130

For command buffer run, add the required processing for allowing `persistent_alloc_indices` to transition only from absent to present across execution steps and must remain unchanged once present.

We may require this transition because the chosen of buffer allocations whose address are in `persistent_alloc_indices` might not be available from the first step, e.g, if persistent allocations are only available after several steps of profiling.  So we need to make sure that command buffer is correctly updated when `persistent_alloc_indices` is changed across steps.

📝 Summary of Changes

- Document that `persistent_alloc_indices` may transition only from absent to
  present across execution steps and must remain unchanged once present.
- Track only whether persistent-allocation information was valid when each
  executor's command buffer was recorded, avoiding an owned vector copy and
  repeated value comparisons.
- Force a full command-buffer update in `CommandBufferThunk::Initialize` when
  persistent allocation information first becomes valid.
- In `ExecuteOnStream`, recover from a recorded/current information-validity
  mismatch by fully updating the command buffer. This covers a
  profiling-skipped update and interleaving with an older execution initialized
  before policy activation.
- Use the public `ShapedSlice` header and its direct BUILD dependency.

🎯 Justification

The persistent allocation policy controls which recorded addresses a command
buffer can safely retain. When the policy first becomes available, an
allocation-filtered update can skip a command whose allocations are now all
persistent, leaving stale addresses in the command buffer. A full update fixes
that transition.

Although the policy transition is logically one-way, executions initialized on
opposite sides of it can interleave on the shared per-executor command buffer.
An Execute-time validity check therefore forces a full update for either
recorded/current mismatch without comparing policy vectors on every step.

🚀 Kind of Contribution

🐛 Bug Fix

📊 Benchmark (for Performance Improvements)

N/A. This is a correctness fix.

🧪 Unit Tests:

Extended `PersistentAllocIndicesBecomingAvailableTriggersUpdate` to verify:

- absent-to-present activation during Initialize triggers exactly one update;
- Execute recovers when the initialization update was skipped;
- an older absent-policy execution and the following present-policy execution
  each restore the correct recorded addresses when they interleave.

Also ran `GpuExecutableTest.CommandBufferAllocationPolicy`.

🧪 Execution Tests:

Ran the focused command-buffer regression and allocation-policy test with
Bazel. All seven configured GPU architecture variants of
`command_buffer_thunk_test` passed, and `gpu_executable_test` passed.

PR #45559: [HLO] Return parse errors instead of CHECK-failing on malformed replica_groups=mesh[...]

Imported from GitHub PR https://github.com/openxla/xla/pull/45559

### Problem
The HLO text parser aborts (via `CHECK`/`CHECK_EQ`) instead of returning a parse error on a malformed `replica_groups=mesh[...]` collective-device list. Because these come straight from user-provided HLO text (`ParseAndReturnUnverifiedModule` and friends), a malformed axis reference or device-id list crashes the process rather than producing a diagnostic. Three sites in `ParseAxisRef`/`ParseMesh`:

1. **Invalid axis ref** — `CHECK(absl::SimpleAtoi(axis_name_or_index, &axis_index))` fires when the axis reference is neither a known mesh axis name nor an integer:
   ```
   replica_groups=mesh['axis_0'=2,'axis_1'=2] {'bogus'}
   ```
2. **Axis index out of bounds** — `CHECK(axis_index >= 0 && axis_index < mesh.axis_names().size())` fires on an out-of-range (or negative) index:
   ```
   replica_groups=mesh['axis_0'=2,'axis_1'=2] {'5'}
   ```
3. **device_ids count mismatch** — `CHECK_EQ(devices.size(), device_ids_array.num_elements())` fires when the `device_ids=(...)` count doesn't match the mesh size:
   ```
   replica_groups=mesh['axis_0'=2,'axis_1'=2, device_ids=(0,1,2)] {'axis_0'}
   ```
   The `Array<int64_t>` is also constructed (allocating the product of the axis sizes) *before* the check, so a huge mesh with a mismatched device list can attempt an unbounded allocation.

### Fix
Return a `TokenError` from each site instead of `CHECK`-failing, matching how the rest of the parser reports malformed input. For (3), the device-id count is validated against an overflow-checked product of the axis sizes *before* the `Array` is allocated, so a mismatched or overflowing mesh yields a parse error rather than a bad-alloc or abort.

Valid `mesh[...]` lists are unaffected (the existing `AllReduceWithMeshAxesReplicaGroupList` round-trip test still passes).

### Tests
Three `HloParserTest` cases assert the malformed inputs above now return an error status instead of crashing.

---
Disclosure: this contribution was authored with an AI coding assistant (Claude) and reviewed before submission.

In this mode, GTE instructions are not printed. Instead, we print the original tuple-shaped operand plus a shape-tree path, e.g. instead of

  ROOT out = (f32[10], f32[20]) tuple(p0#0, p0#1)

[XLA:GPU] Refactor DotMerger to use DotOperandDims and ShapeTracker.

This change enhances the capabilities of the DotMerger pass. Instead of relying on specific patterns like DotDecomposer's normal form, the new implementation uses `DotOperandDims` to track and categorize dimensions (Batch, Contracting, NonContracting) and `ShapeTracker` to generate the necessary transformation instructions (transpose, reshape, bitcast) to align operand shapes for merging.

Key improvements include:
-   Support for merging dots with different layouts on the shared or concatenated operands.
-   Ability to handle dots with arbitrary batch, contracting, and non-contracting dimension orders.
-   Merging of dots even when the non-shared operands have multiple non-contracting dimensions.
-   Improved handling of dependencies by merging only mutually unreachable dots within an equivalence class.
-   Simplification of consumer chains (transposes, reshapes, bitcasts) after the merged dot's output is sliced.

The equivalence classes are now built based on the shared operand's source instruction and the categorized dimensions at that source, allowing for more flexible grouping. The merging process involves transforming the operands to a common shape/layout before concatenation and then creating a single large dot. The results are then sliced and potentially transformed back to match the original dot outputs.

[XLA:CPU] Safely handle shared branch computations in LayoutAssignment.
In layout assignment, when a conditional has branch computations with layout mismatches, they are recorded in conditional_mismatch_. Because optimization passes can cause multiple conditional instructions to share the same branch computation, using InsertOrDie to insert into conditional_mismatch_ can crash on duplicate keys. This replaces InsertOrDie with try_emplace to safely handle shared branch computations.

### Change
Cleanup in `GetAbstractReplicaGroups` (the send/recv branch). The source-target-pairs lookup assigned `Map::at()`'s returned `const std::string&` into a `std::optional<std::string>` and then `CHECK`'d `has_value()`, which is always true (the optional is constructed from a live reference). PR #45401: [HLO] Rewrite a scalar-operand gather to a broadcast in GatherSimplifier

Imported from GitHub PR https://github.com/openxla/xla/pull/45401

### Problem
`GatherSimplifier` crashed on a gather from a scalar (rank-0) operand: such a gather has empty `offset_dims`, which `IsSimplifiedGather` dereferenced out of bounds (and the pass could otherwise loop without making progress).

### Fix
Rather than treating a scalar-operand gather as already-simplified (which just skips it), eliminate it — mirroring `ScatterSimplifier`:
- `IsSimplifiedGather` returns `false` for a rank-0 operand (`operand_rank == 0`), which also avoids the out-of-bounds `offset_dims` dereference.
- `ExpandInstruction` rewrites a rank-0-operand gather into a `broadcast` of the scalar operand to the result shape (matching the existing zero-slice-size → broadcast path), so the gather is removed instead of being left in place.

Behavior is unchanged for all other gathers.

### Test
`RewritesScalarOperandGatherToBroadcast` asserts a scalar-operand gather is rewritten to `broadcast(%operand), dimensions={}`.

[HLO] Fix out-of-bounds read for a scalar-operand gather in GatherSimplifier

IsSimplifiedGather dereferenced *offset_dims().begin() and *rbegin() guarded
only by collapsed_slice_dims().empty(), never by offset_dims() being non-empty.
A gather from a rank-0 operand has empty offset_dims, so both derefs read out
of bounds of an empty RepeatedField (UB / garbage bool; a wrong false also

(the degenerate form for a scalar operand), mirroring ScatterSimplifier's

PR #45507: [ROCM] Fix hipBLASLt lowering of scaled-dot fusions with reshaped operands

Imported from GitHub PR https://github.com/openxla/xla/pull/45507

**Summary**
Fixes the hipBLASLt MX backend so it correctly lowers Triton GEMM fusions containing a scaled-dot when the fusion's external operands have a different rank than the scaled-dot operands (the in-graph block-scaled quantization case).

**Changes**
Refactor operand handling in hipblaslt.cc: Replaced the boolean IsScaledDotFusion helper with GetScaledDotFromFusion, which returns the scaled-dot instruction directly, removing duplicated lookups across IsSupported, GetSupportedConfigs, and ApplyConfig.

Correctly map fusion parameters to scaled-dot operands: Instead of assuming operands 0–3 map directly, ApplyConfig now walks forward from each fusion parameter to the scaled-dot operand it feeds. Trivial rank-changing ops (bitcast/reshape) on the path are allowed and re-applied so the custom-call operands stay consistent with the dot dimension numbers. Added TF_RET_CHECKs to guard against unexpected ops, non-root scaled-dots, and unmapped operands.

**Improve diagnostics**
Promoted several silent VLOG(2) failure paths to LOG(WARNING) so unsupported/failed lowerings are visible.

**Tests**: Added MxFp8ReshapedOperandsCorrectness covering the reshaped/higher-rank operand path, and cleaned up the scaled-dot execution test.

PiperOrigin-RevId: 946947421

Lower xtile.scan to Triton.

This change introduces a pattern to transform `xtile::ScanOp` into `ttir::ScanOp`. There are three gaps to bridge:

- The combine region of `xtile.scan` operates on rank N-1 tensors, but that of `tt.scan` operates on scalars. So we need to scalarize the types when cloning the combine region.
- `xtile.scan` supports initial values, but `tt.scan` does not. So we need to broadcast them and add them to the result of `tt.scan`.
- Finally, `tt.scan` does not return final carry values. We extract them by slicing the scan result.

The change also introduces support for left-masking `xtile.extract/insert`. This is needed for reverse scan when the tile size is not a multiple of the scan dimension size.

[XLA:Service] Fix ASan stack-use-after-scope in HLO verifiers and tests

When verifier classes (e.g., `ShapeVerifier`, `InstructionVerifier`) are instantiated directly with inline temporary `HloVerifierOpts{}` rvalues in standalone tests or utilities, storing options by const reference (`const HloVerifierOpts& opts_`) causes a dangling reference hazard. When the temporary expression terminates, the options object is destroyed on the stack, resulting in an AddressSanitizer (ASan) `stack-use-after-scope` abort upon dereferencing member fields like `opts_.layout_sensitive`.

This change fixes the lifetime vulnerability by:
1. Updating `ShapeVerifier` and `InstructionVerifier` to safely manage `HloVerifierOpts` lifetimes rather than retaining vulnerable const references to temporary options.
2. Updating `hlo_verifier_test.cc` to bind `HloVerifierOpts` to local lvalue variables before passing them to `ShapeVerifier`, ensuring the options outlive the verifier instance during test execution.
